perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party undertakes to keep the Information confidential and not to disclose it to any third parties and to avoid the risk of the information being disclosed to any third parties. The Receiving Party ag rees not to use the In formation in any other way than set forth in this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party undertakes to keep the Information confidential and not to disclose it to any third parties. However, if legally compelled by subpoena, court order, or other legal process, the Receiving Party may disclose the Information after providing 24-hour notice to the Inventor/Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces a shortened notification period for disclosing confidential information when legally compelled. This can conflict with laws or regulations that require a longer period for the disclosing party to respond and potentially challenge the disclosure.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, Rule 45(d)(2)(B)
perturbation.law_explanation: FRCP 45(d)(2)(B) implies a reasonable time for response and objection to subpoenas. A 24-hour notice period is unlikely to be considered reasonable, especially when dealing with sensitive confidential information, thus creating a conflict with the spirit and intent of the rule regarding adequate time for response.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 45. Subpoena | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 45. Subpoena (a) In General. (1) Form and Contents. (A) Requirements—In General. Every subpoena must: (i) state the court from which it issued; (ii) state the title of the action and its civil-action number; (iii) command each person to whom it is directed to do the following at a specified time and place: attend and testify; produce designated documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things in that person's possession, custody, or control; or permit the inspection of premises; and (iv) set out the text of Rule 45(d) and (e). (B) Command to Attend a Deposition—Notice of the Recording Method. A subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition must state the method for recording the testimony. (C) Combining or Separating a Command to Produce or to Permit Inspection; Specifying the Form for Electronically Stored Information. A command to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things or to permit the inspection of premises may be included in a subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition, hearing, or trial, or may be set out in a separate subpoena. A subpoena may specify the form or forms in which electronically stored information is to be produced. (D) Command to Produce; Included Obligations. A command in a subpoena to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things requires the responding person to permit inspection, copying, testing, or sampling of the materials. (2) Issuing Court. A subpoena must issue from the court where the action is pending. (3) Issued by Whom. The clerk must issue a subpoena, signed but otherwise in blank, to a party who requests it. That party must complete it before service. An attorney also may issue and sign a subpoena if the attorney is authorized to practice in the issuing court. (4) Notice to Other Parties Before Service. If th
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Federal Rules of Civil Procedure Skip to main content Official websites use .gov A .gov website belongs to an official government organization in the United States. Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. Your browser does not support Javascript.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a 24-hour notice period for disclosing confidential information when legally compelled. The Law Explanation states that FRCP 45(d)(2)(B) implies a reasonable time for response and objection to subpoenas, and that a 24-hour notice is unlikely to be considered reasonable. Scraped Law Snippet 1, which refers to Rule 45, doesn't specify a concrete timeframe, but the Law Explanation argues that 24 hours is likely insufficient to comply with the implied requirements of the rule. This creates a legal contradiction, because the altered contract potentially undermines the ability of the disclosing party to adequately respond or object to the subpoena within a legally reasonable timeframe, as suggested by the legal explanation of FRCP 45. This falls under the Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction definition.

perturbation.original_text: If not otherwise agreed in writing, the Receiving Party shall, at the request of the Inventor/ Disclosing Party, immediately return all material describing the Information, as stipulated in provision 1.
perturbation.changed_text: If not otherwise agreed in writing, the Receiving Party shall, at the request of the Inventor/ Disclosing Party, return all material describing the Information, as stipulated in provision 1, within 90 days of the request.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'immediately return' to 'within 90 days' creates a delay that could be detrimental to the Disclosing Party, particularly if the information is time-sensitive or critical to ongoing operations. Some jurisdictions might have laws requiring prompt return or destruction of confidential information upon request under certain circumstances.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Trade Secret laws (e.g., Uniform Trade Secrets Act)
perturbation.law_explanation: While not directly specifying a time frame for return, trade secret laws emphasize the protection of proprietary information. A 90-day delay in returning critical trade secret materials could be seen as a failure to take reasonable measures to protect the trade secret, potentially undermining its legal protection. This conflicts with the obligation to safeguard trade secrets effectively.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 404 | Berkman Klein Center Skip to the main content Page Cannot Be Found Brave traveler, your journey on our site has brought you to an abrupt dead end. Perhaps you wish to explore one of these other options? About Us Get Involved Events Community Publications Projects & Tools Last updated Mar 27, 2024
perturbation.justification: The change from 'immediately return' to 'within 90 days' introduces a significant delay that could undermine the protection of trade secrets. While the scraped snippets do not directly address the return timeframe, the Law Explanation highlights that trade secret laws emphasize the protection of proprietary information, and delaying the return of critical materials could be seen as a failure to take reasonable measures to protect the trade secret. This aligns with the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition because the modified text creates a time-sensitive obligation that may violate state law regarding trade secret protection by not ensuring immediate safeguarding.

perturbation.original_text: This agreement does not cover: Technical information or other information which, at the time when the Inventor/ Disclosing Party provided the Receiving Party with the Information, was considered publicly known or later on becomes publicly known without the Receiving Party being liable for making it publicly known.
perturbation.changed_text: This agreement does not cover: Technical information or other information which, at the time when the Inventor/ Disclosing Party provided the Receiving Party with the Information, was considered publicly known. Any information becoming publicly known will still be covered by this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This alteration attempts to bind the Receiving Party to confidentiality even for information that becomes publicly known, which is generally unenforceable and contradicts established legal precedent regarding trade secrets and confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: Comment b of Section 757 explicitly states that a trade secret must be secret. Once information is generally known or readily ascertainable, it loses its trade secret status. The modified agreement attempts to circumvent this principle by maintaining confidentiality obligations even after public disclosure, directly contradicting the fundamental requirement of secrecy for trade secret protection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 404 | Berkman Klein Center Skip to the main content Page Cannot Be Found Brave traveler, your journey on our site has brought you to an abrupt dead end. Perhaps you wish to explore one of these other options? About Us Get Involved Events Community Publications Projects & Tools Last updated Mar 27, 2024
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the principle that information loses trade secret protection when it becomes publicly known. The modified text states that 'Any information becoming publicly known will still be covered by this agreement,' which attempts to maintain confidentiality even after public disclosure. The Law Explanation confirms that Section 757 comment b states that a trade secret must be secret. Snippet 2 has a 404 error. Snippet 1 has no information.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party is held liable to the Inventor/Disclosing Party for damages in the event of breach of this Agreement. The Receiving Party is also held liable in case of breach of the Agreement by third parties to whom the Receiving Party has handed over the Information wholly or partly.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party is held liable to the Inventor/Disclosing Party for damages in the event of breach of this Agreement. The Receiving Party is also held liable in case of breach of the Agreement by third parties to whom the Receiving Party has handed over the Information wholly or partly, regardless of whether the Receiving Party was negligent.
perturbation.explanation: This modification imposes strict liability on the Receiving Party for breaches by third parties, even if the Receiving Party was not negligent. This could be deemed unenforceable as it contradicts principles of fairness and reasonable foreseeability in determining liability.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Principles of Tort Law - Negligence
perturbation.law_explanation: Standard tort law, as reflected in Restatement (Second) of Torts § 281, generally requires a showing of negligence for liability to be established. Imposing liability regardless of negligence deviates from this fundamental principle. Many jurisdictions require some degree of fault or negligence to hold a party responsible for the actions of third parties, particularly if there is no direct control.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: negligence | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you negligence Negligence is the failure to behave with the level of care that a reasonable person would have exercised under the same circumstances. Either a person’s actions or omissions of actions can be found negligent. The omission of actions is considered negligent only when the person had a duty to act (e.g., a duty to help someone because of one’s own previous conduct). Negligence is a foundational concept of tort law. Some primary factors to consider in ascertaining whether a person’s conduct lacks reasonable care are the foreseeable likelihood that the conduct would result in harm, the foreseeable severity of the harm, and the burden of precautions necessary to eliminate or reduce the risk of harm. The following five elements may typically be required to prove negligence: The existence of a legal duty that the defendant owed the plaintiff Defendant’s breach of that duty Harm to the plaintiff Defendant’s actions are the proximate cause of harm to the plaintiff Defendant’s actions are the cause-in-fact of harm to the plaintiff Legal Duty of Care Generally, members of society have entered a social contract that includes a duty to not cause harm to others. When considering legal duty as an element of negligence, there is a duty to act reasonably. When a jury is asked to determine if a defendant’s actions were reasonable, the standard is what a reasonable person would have done in the defendant’s situation. The following is a non-exhaustive list of ways to determine if the defendant owed a duty of care to the plaintiff: The defendant engaged in the creation of risk which resulted in the plaintiff’s harm Voluntary undertaking: the defendant volunteered to protect the plaintiff from harm, possibly preventing others from protecting the plaintiff Knowledge: the defendant knows or should know that their conduct will harm the plaintiff Business/voluntary relationships: certain relationships—such as business owner and customer, innkeeper and guest, landowner and lessee, doctor and patient—create duties of care Breach of Duty of Care Some courts in the United States use the Hand Formula (created by Judge Learned Hand in United States v. Carroll Towing ) to determine if there was a breach of the duty of care to the plaintiff. If B < PL, then the defendant breached the duty of care. B=burden of taking precautions P=probability of loss L=severity of loss (personal loss, not societal loss) If the burden of taking such precautions is less than the probability of injury multiplied by the severity of the resulting injury, then the defending party breached their duty of care to the plaintiff and may be liable for the plaintiff’s injuries, if the remaining elements of negligence can be proven. This formula considers economic costs to the defendant in its approach to assessing liability. The idea is not to eliminate the risk of harm entirely, for complete elimination of risk would result in a high economical burden for the defendant that would then trickle down to costs for the consumer. Instead, the defendant is encouraged to take precautions that reduce either the probability of harm or severity of possible harm, such that their burden is less than the probability of harm multiplied by the severity of potential harm. Harm to the Plaintiff Typically, harm to the plaintiff must be either bodily harm or harm to property ( personal property or real property ). Harm that is solely economic usually will not satisfy this element in proving negligence. Some states recognize emotional distress as harm to the plaintiff, even if the harm is purely mental and not physical. Proximate Cause To satisfy proximate cause, the defendant’s breach of the duty of care must be the actual cause of the harm to the plaintiff. One way to assess proximate cause is to evaluate the foreseeability of the defendant’s actions resulting in harm. For example, if the d
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces strict liability on the Receiving Party for breaches by third parties, regardless of negligence. This directly contradicts the standard tort law principle, where negligence is typically required to establish liability, as outlined in the Law Explanation. The scraped law snippet from LII defines negligence as 'the failure to behave with the level of care that a reasonable person would have exercised under the same circumstances' and emphasizes that negligence is a 'foundational concept of tort law.' By removing the requirement of negligence, the changed text creates a legal contradiction, potentially leading to an unenforceable agreement because it deviates from established legal principles regarding liability.

perturbation.original_text: Any disputes, arising out or relating to this Agreement, shall be settled by the ordinary courts in the Inventor's/Disclosing Party's home country and according to the legislation of the Inventor 's/Disclosing Party's home country.
perturbation.changed_text: Any disputes, arising out or relating to this Agreement, shall be settled by binding arbitration in the Inventor's/Disclosing Party's home country, waiving all rights to appeal any arbitration decision.
perturbation.explanation: This change forces binding arbitration with no appeal rights. While arbitration is generally acceptable, eliminating all appeal rights can be problematic, especially if the arbitration process is unfair or biased. Courts often retain limited jurisdiction to review arbitration awards for procedural fairness or manifest disregard of the law.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Federal Arbitration Act (9 U.S.C. § 10) outlines specific grounds for vacating an arbitration award, such as corruption, fraud, or evident partiality of the arbitrator. Eliminating all rights to appeal effectively prevents a party from challenging an award even under these circumstances, which can conflict with fundamental principles of due process and fairness ensured by the FAA.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 9 U.S. Code § 10 - Same; vacation; grounds; rehearing | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 9 U.S. Code § 10 - Same; vacation; grounds; rehearing U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) In any of the following cases the United States court in and for the district wherein the award was made may make an order vacating the award upon the application of any party to the arbitration— (1) where the award was procured by corruption, fraud, or undue means; (2) where there was evident partiality or corruption in the arbitrators, or either of them; (3) where the arbitrators were guilty of misconduct in refusing to postpone the hearing, upon sufficient cause shown, or in refusing to hear evidence pertinent and material to the controversy; or of any other misbehavior by which the rights of any party have been prejudiced; or (4) where the arbitrators exceeded their powers, or so imperfectly executed them that a mutual, final, and definite award upon the subject matter submitted was not made. (b) If an award is vacated and the time within which the agreement required the award to be made has not expired, the court may, in its discretion, direct a rehearing by the arbitrators. (c) The United States district court for the district wherein an award was made that was issued pursuant to section 580 of title 5 may make an order vacating the award upon the application of a person, other than a party to the arbitration, who is adversely affected or aggrieved by the award, if the use of arbitration or the award is clearly inconsistent with the factors set forth in section 572 of title 5 . (July 30, 1947, ch. 392, 61 Stat. 672 ; Pub. L. 101–552, § 5 , Nov. 15, 1990 , 104 Stat. 2745 ; Pub. L. 102–354, § 5(b)(4) , Aug. 26, 1992 , 106 Stat. 946 ; Pub. L. 107–169, § 1 , May 7, 2002 , 116 Stat. 132 .) Derivation Act Feb. 12, 1925, ch. 213, § 10, 43 Stat. 885 . Editorial Notes Amendments 2002—Subsec. (a)(1) to

United States Code, 2011 Edition Title 9 - ARBITRATION CHAPTER 1 - GENERAL PROVISIONS Sec. 10 - Same; vacation; grounds; rehearing From the U.S. Government Publishing Office, www.gpo.gov §10. Same; vacation; grounds; rehearing (a) In any of the following cases the United States court in and for the district wherein the award was made may make an order vacating the award upon the application of any party to the arbitration— (1) where the award was procured by corruption, fraud, or undue means; (2) where there was evident partiality or corruption in the arbitrators, or either of them; (3) where the arbitrators were guilty of misconduct in refusing to postpone the hearing, upon sufficient cause shown, or in refusing to hear evidence pertinent and material to the controversy; or of any other misbehavior by which the rights of any party have been prejudiced; or (4) where the arbitrators exceeded their powers, or so imperfectly executed them that a mutual, final, and definite award upon the subject matter submitted was not made. (b) If an award is vacated and the time within which the agreement required the award to be made has not expired, the court may, in its discretion, direct a rehearing by the arbitrators. (c) The United States district court for the district wherein an award was made that was issued pursuant to section 580 of title 5 may make an order vacating the award upon the application of a person, other than a party to the arbitration, who is adversely affected or aggrieved by the award, if the use of arbitration or the award is clearly inconsistent with the factors set forth in section 572 of title 5. (July 30, 1947, ch. 392, 61 Stat. 672; Pub. L. 101–552, §5, Nov. 15, 1990, 104 Stat. 2745; Pub. L. 102–354, §5(b)(4), Aug. 26, 1992, 106 Stat. 946; Pub. L. 107–169, §1, May 7, 2002, 116 Stat. 132.) Derivation Act Feb. 12, 1925, ch. 213, §10, 43 Stat. 885. Amendments 2002 —Subsec. (a)(1) to (4). Pub. L. 107–169, §1(1)–(3), substituted “where” for “Where” and realigned margins in pars. (1) to (4), and substituted a semicolon for period at end in pars. (1
perturbation.justification: The modified text mandates binding arbitration with no appeal rights, directly conflicting with the Federal Arbitration Act as evidenced by 9 U.S.C. § 10, which outlines specific grounds for vacating an arbitration award (e.g., corruption, fraud, evident partiality). By waiving all appeal rights, the modified text prevents a party from challenging an award even under circumstances explicitly recognized by federal law, thus creating a contradiction. Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2 both state the grounds for vacating an arbitration award in 9 U.S.C. § 10, including "where the award was procured by corruption, fraud, or undue means;" and "where there was evident partiality or corruption in the arbitrators". The altered contract language removes access to legal recourse provided by the FAA.
